revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Hōtō]][[ja:宝塔]][[nl:Hōtō]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11452357]] Wikidata name: hōtō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I think Stupa might fit better than Pagoda due to being one level tall but not sure[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:39, 6 April 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}}
{{Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}} {{nihongo|'''Treasure Pagoda'''|宝塔|hōtō}} has the following two meanings: * It is one architectural style of Buddhist pagodas, referring to a single-storied tower with a cylindrical body. (This article explains this meaning.) * It is also an honorific term for Buddhist pagodas in general. ---- == Form and Significance of the Pagoda == A ''pagoda'' (宝塔, hōtō) is one architectural style of Buddhist pagodas. Although the term “pagoda” is sometimes used honorifically to refer to pagodas in general regardless of form or style, in the terminology of Japanese architectural history it refers specifically to a single-storied pagoda having a cylindrical core (tower body) topped with a flat, square roof<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本石造美術辞典|last=川勝|first=政太郎|date=1978|publisher=東京堂|page=347}}; {{Cite book|title=古建築の基礎知識|last=前久夫|date=1986|publisher=光村推古書院|page=61}}</ref>. The upper portion of the cylinder is rounded off in a style known as “kame-bara” (turtle-belly). A row of ornamental rings (相輪) similar to those on multi-storied pagodas is placed on top of the roof. Pagodas may be constructed of wood, metal, or stone, although extant examples of wooden pagodas are extremely rare. In Japan, the oldest surviving example of a wooden pagoda is a bronze pagoda from the Ikegami Honmon-ji complex (built around the first year of the Hōan era, late Heian period)<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本石造美術辞典|last=川勝|first=政太郎|date=1978|publisher=東京堂|page=347}}</ref>. Note that a two-storied structure in which a flat, circular upper story is mounted on top of an initial flat, square story (known as a “Tahōtō”) is distinguished from the pagoda described here; this distinction is merely conventional.
== Examples of Pagodas == === Wooden Pagodas === [[File:鳳閣寺廟塔02.JPG|thumb|Sample image: a stone pagoda (at {{ill|Hōkaku-ji|ja|鳳閣寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkaku-ji|qq=|1=Hōkaku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11675305}} in [[:en:Kurotaki, Nara|Kurotaki Village]], [[:en:Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino-gun]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]) – note that this image is miscaptioned as “stone pagoda.”]] * [[:en:Ikegami Honmon-ji|Ikegami Honmon-ji]] (located in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], designated an Important Cultural Property) – built in 1828 (Bunsei 11).
=== Indoor Mini-Pagodas === * {{ill|Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|ja|慈光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|qq=|1=Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11494981}} (Saitama Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in 1556 (Tenmon 25). * {{ill|Seikai-ji (Ina City)|ja|性海寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seikai-ji (Ina City)|qq=|1=Seikai-ji (Ina City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11491604}} (Aichi Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) * {{ill|Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|ja|長蔵寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|qq=|1=Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q113498452}} (Gifu Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) * Honmon-ji (in Tokyo, Important Cultural Property) – built in 1830 (Bunsei 13); this pagoda is contained within the Honmon-ji complex.
=== Stone Pagodas === Stone pagodas occupy a major section of the field of [[Stone Art]]. Some stone pagodas feature carved door-like panels or images of two figures (the Manyo Rulai and Shakyamuni Tathāgata) on the cylindrical body. Regional examples include the {{ill|Kunō-tō|ja|国東塔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunō-tō|qq=|1=Kunō-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420973}} in {{ill|Kunisaki, Ōita|ja|国東|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisaki, Ōita|qq=|1=Kunisaki, Ōita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420970}}, Ōita Prefecture, and the {{ill|Akazaki-tō|ja|赤碕塔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazaki-tō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]]. * [[:en:Kurama-dera|Kurama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Kyoto, National Treasure) – an inscribed Buddhist scripture tube bearing the inscription of the Hōan era (【1120】) was discovered buried in the soil beneath the tower. It is considered the oldest extant example of a stone pagoda. * Ruins of {{ill|Kande-ji|ja|関寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kande-ji|qq=|1=Seki-dera Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655708}}: “Ushi-tō” (Stone Pagoda), (in Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – from the late Heian period; 3.3 m tall with an octagonal roof; the ornamental ring atop the roof was added later. It, together with the Kurama-dera pagoda, Ranks among the oldest. * The ruins of the pagoda at {{ill|Mangan-ji|ja|満願寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mangan-ji|qq=|1=Mangan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563954}} (in Takashima City, Shiga Prefecture) – from the early Kamakura period; 4 m tall; the ornamental ring was added later. * [[:en:Jōdo-ji|Jōdo-ji (Onomichi)]] Pagoda (in Onomichi, Hiroshima Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the date of the first year of the Kōan era (【1278】); 2.8 m tall; built using rammed earth on a dais. * {{ill|Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|ja|西明寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|qq=|1=Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628430}} Pagoda (in Kōra-cho, Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the 2nd year of the Jiagen era (【1304】). * [[:en:Ishiyama-dera|Ishiyama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture) – from the mid-Kamakura period; currently about 2 m tall, though originally estimated at about 3 m. * Ishiyama-dera “Mekakushi-iwa” (in Shiga Prefecture) – from the Heian period. * {{ill|Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|ja|安養寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|qq=|1=Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451679}} (with a pagoda attributed to [[:en:Jien|Jien]]) (in Kyoto, Important Cultural Property) – from the mid-Kamakura period; approximately 3 m tall with carvings of the Manyo and Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddhas on the tower body. * [[:en:Rokuharamitsu-ji|Roppōmitsu-ji]], Agoya-tsuka (in Kyoto Prefecture) – from the Kamakura period. * {{ill|Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|ja|長楽寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|qq=|1=Chōraku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653105}} Pagoda (in Ota City, Gunma Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in the 2nd year of Kenchi (【1276】).
== References == * Ishida Shigesaku, “Pagoda – Tower and Tower-Ba (Stūba)”, Japanese Art 77, 1972, Shibundo. * Nakani Tō, “Overview of Japanese Pagodas”, 1970, Bunka-dō. * Kawakatsu Masatarō, “Introduction to Stone Art”, 1967, Shakai Shisōsha.
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Prabhutaratna|Manyo Rulai]] * [[:en:Cloisonné|Seven Treasures]] * {{ill|Kunō-tō|ja|国東塔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunō-tō|qq=|1=Kunō-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420973}} * {{ill|Akagi-tō|ja|赤城塔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagi-tō|qq=}} * [[:en:Tahōtō|Tahōtō]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Hōtō]][[ja:宝塔]][[nl:Hōtō]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11452357]] Wikidata name: hōtō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I think Stupa might fit better than Pagoda due to being one level tall but not sure[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:39, 6 April 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}}
{{Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}} {{nihongo|'''Treasure Pagoda'''|宝塔|hōtō}} has the following two meanings: * It is one architectural style of Buddhist pagodas, referring to a single-storied tower with a cylindrical body. (This article explains this meaning.) * It is also an honorific term for Buddhist pagodas in general. ---- == Form and Significance of the Pagoda == A ''pagoda'' (宝塔, hōtō) is one architectural style of Buddhist pagodas. Although the term “pagoda” is sometimes used honorifically to refer to pagodas in general regardless of form or style, in the terminology of Japanese architectural history it refers specifically to a single-storied pagoda having a cylindrical core (tower body) topped with a flat, square roof<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本石造美術辞典|last=川勝|first=政太郎|date=1978|publisher=東京堂|page=347}}; {{Cite book|title=古建築の基礎知識|last=前久夫|date=1986|publisher=光村推古書院|page=61}}</ref>. The upper portion of the cylinder is rounded off in a style known as “kame-bara” (turtle-belly). A row of ornamental rings (相輪) similar to those on multi-storied pagodas is placed on top of the roof. Pagodas may be constructed of wood, metal, or stone, although extant examples of wooden pagodas are extremely rare. In Japan, the oldest surviving example of a wooden pagoda is a bronze pagoda from the Ikegami Honmon-ji complex (built around the first year of the Hōan era, late Heian period)<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本石造美術辞典|last=川勝|first=政太郎|date=1978|publisher=東京堂|page=347}}</ref>. Note that a two-storied structure in which a flat, circular upper story is mounted on top of an initial flat, square story (known as a “Tahōtō”) is distinguished from the pagoda described here; this distinction is merely conventional.
== Examples of Pagodas == === Wooden Pagodas === [[File:鳳閣寺廟塔02.JPG|thumb|Sample image: a stone pagoda (at {{ill|Hōkaku-ji|ja|鳳閣寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkaku-ji|qq=|1=Hōkaku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11675305}} in [[:en:Kurotaki, Nara|Kurotaki Village]], [[:en:Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino-gun]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]) – note that this image is miscaptioned as “stone pagoda.”]] * [[:en:Ikegami Honmon-ji|Ikegami Honmon-ji]] (located in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], designated an Important Cultural Property) – built in 1828 (Bunsei 11).
=== Indoor Mini-Pagodas === * {{ill|Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|ja|慈光寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|qq=|1=Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11494981}} (Saitama Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in 1556 (Tenmon 25). * {{ill|Seikai-ji (Ina City)|ja|性海寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seikai-ji (Ina City)|qq=|1=Seikai-ji (Ina City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11491604}} (Aichi Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) * {{ill|Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|ja|長蔵寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|qq=|1=Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q113498452}} (Gifu Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) * Honmon-ji (in Tokyo, Important Cultural Property) – built in 1830 (Bunsei 13); this pagoda is contained within the Honmon-ji complex.
=== Stone Pagodas === Stone pagodas occupy a major section of the field of [[Stone Art]]. Some stone pagodas feature carved door-like panels or images of two figures (the Manyo Rulai and Shakyamuni Tathāgata) on the cylindrical body. Regional examples include the {{ill|Kunō-tō|ja|国東塔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunō-tō|qq=|1=Kunō-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420973}} in {{ill|Kunisaki, Ōita|ja|国東|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisaki, Ōita|qq=|1=Kunisaki, Ōita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420970}}, Ōita Prefecture, and the {{ill|Akazaki-tō|ja|赤碕塔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazaki-tō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]]. * [[:en:Kurama-dera|Kurama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Kyoto, National Treasure) – an inscribed Buddhist scripture tube bearing the inscription of the Hōan era (【1120】) was discovered buried in the soil beneath the tower. It is considered the oldest extant example of a stone pagoda. * Ruins of {{ill|Kande-ji|ja|関寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kande-ji|qq=|1=Seki-dera Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655708}}: “Ushi-tō” (Stone Pagoda), (in Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – from the late Heian period; 3.3 m tall with an octagonal roof; the ornamental ring atop the roof was added later. It, together with the Kurama-dera pagoda, Ranks among the oldest. * The ruins of the pagoda at {{ill|Mangan-ji|ja|満願寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mangan-ji|qq=|1=Mangan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563954}} (in Takashima City, Shiga Prefecture) – from the early Kamakura period; 4 m tall; the ornamental ring was added later. * [[:en:Jōdo-ji|Jōdo-ji (Onomichi)]] Pagoda (in Onomichi, Hiroshima Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the date of the first year of the Kōan era (【1278】); 2.8 m tall; built using rammed earth on a dais. * {{ill|Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|ja|西明寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|qq=|1=Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628430}} Pagoda (in Kōra-cho, Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the 2nd year of the Jiagen era (【1304】). * [[:en:Ishiyama-dera|Ishiyama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture) – from the mid-Kamakura period; currently about 2 m tall, though originally estimated at about 3 m. * Ishiyama-dera “Mekakushi-iwa” (in Shiga Prefecture) – from the Heian period. * {{ill|Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|ja|安養寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|qq=|1=Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451679}} (with a pagoda attributed to [[:en:Jien|Jien]]) (in Kyoto, Important Cultural Property) – from the mid-Kamakura period; approximately 3 m tall with carvings of the Manyo and Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddhas on the tower body. * [[:en:Rokuharamitsu-ji|Roppōmitsu-ji]], Agoya-tsuka (in Kyoto Prefecture) – from the Kamakura period. * {{ill|Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|ja|長楽寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|qq=|1=Chōraku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653105}} Pagoda (in Ota City, Gunma Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in the 2nd year of Kenchi (【1276】).
== References == * Ishida Shigesaku, “Pagoda – Tower and Tower-Ba (Stūba)”, Japanese Art 77, 1972, Shibundo. * Nakani Tō, “Overview of Japanese Pagodas”, 1970, Bunka-dō. * Kawakatsu Masatarō, “Introduction to Stone Art”, 1967, Shakai Shisōsha.

It is considered the oldest extant example of a stone pagoda. * Ruins of {{ill|Kande-ji|ja|関寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kande-ji|1=Seki-dera Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655708}}: “Ushi-tō” (Stone Pagoda), (in Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – from the late Heian period; 3.3 m tall with an octagonal roof; the ornamental ring atop the roof was added later. It, together with the Kurama-dera pagoda, Ranks among the oldest. * The ruins of the pagoda at {{ill|Mangan-ji|ja|満願寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mangan-ji|1=Mangan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563954}} (in Takashima City, Shiga Prefecture) – from the early Kamakura period; 4 m tall; the ornamental ring was added later. * [[:en:Jōdo-ji|Jōdo-ji (Onomichi)]] Pagoda (in Onomichi, Hiroshima Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the date of the first year of the Kōan era (【1278】); 2.8 m tall; built using rammed earth on a dais. * {{ill|Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|ja|西明寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|1=Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628430}} Pagoda (in Kōra-cho, Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the 2nd year of the Jiagen era (【1304】). * [[:en:Ishiyama-dera|Ishiyama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture) – from the mid-Kamakura period; currently about 2 m tall, though originally estimated at about 3 m. * Ishiyama-dera “Mekakushi-iwa” (in Shiga Prefecture) – from the Heian period. * {{ill|Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|ja|安養寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|1=Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451679}} (with a pagoda attributed to [[:en:Jien|Jien]]) (in Kyoto, Important Cultural Property) – from the mid-Kamakura period; approximately 3 m tall with carvings of the Manyo and Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddhas on the tower body. * [[:en:Rokuharamitsu-ji|Roppōmitsu-ji]], Agoya-tsuka (in Kyoto Prefecture) – from the Kamakura period. * {{ill|Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|ja|長楽寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|1=Chōraku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653105}} Pagoda (in Ota City, Gunma Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in the 2nd year of Kenchi (【1276】).
== References == * Ishida Shigesaku, “Pagoda – Tower and Tower-Ba (Stūba)”, Japanese Art 77, 1972, Shibundo. * Nakani Tō, “Overview of Japanese Pagodas”, 1970, Bunka-dō. * Kawakatsu Masatarō, “Introduction to Stone Art”, 1967, Shakai Shisōsha.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Hōtō]][[ja:宝塔]][[nl:Hōtō]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11452357]] Wikidata name: hōtō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I think Stupa might fit better than Pagoda due to being one level tall but not sure[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:39, 6 April 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}}
{{Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}} {{nihongo|'''Treasure Pagoda'''|宝塔|hōtō}} has the following two meanings: * It is one architectural style of Buddhist pagodas, referring to a single-storied tower with a cylindrical body. (This article explains this meaning.) * It is also an honorific term for Buddhist pagodas in general. ---- == Form and Significance of the Pagoda == A ''pagoda'' (宝塔, hōtō) is one architectural style of Buddhist pagodas. Although the term “pagoda” is sometimes used honorifically to refer to pagodas in general regardless of form or style, in the terminology of Japanese architectural history it refers specifically to a single-storied pagoda having a cylindrical core (tower body) topped with a flat, square roof<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本石造美術辞典|last=川勝|first=政太郎|date=1978|publisher=東京堂|page=347}}; {{Cite book|title=古建築の基礎知識|last=前久夫|date=1986|publisher=光村推古書院|page=61}}</ref>. The upper portion of the cylinder is rounded off in a style known as “kame-bara” (turtle-belly). A row of ornamental rings (相輪) similar to those on multi-storied pagodas is placed on top of the roof. Pagodas may be constructed of wood, metal, or stone, although extant examples of wooden pagodas are extremely rare. In Japan, the oldest surviving example of a wooden pagoda is a bronze pagoda from the Ikegami Honmon-ji complex (built around the first year of the Hōan era, late Heian period)<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本石造美術辞典|last=川勝|first=政太郎|date=1978|publisher=東京堂|page=347}}</ref>. Note that a two-storied structure in which a flat, circular upper story is mounted on top of an initial flat, square story (known as a “Tahōtō”) is distinguished from the pagoda described here; this distinction is merely conventional.
== Examples of Pagodas == === Wooden Pagodas === [[File:鳳閣寺廟塔02.JPG|thumb|Sample image: a stone pagoda (at {{ill|Hōkaku-ji|ja|鳳閣寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkaku-ji|1=Hōkaku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11675305}} in [[:en:Kurotaki, Nara|Kurotaki Village]], [[:en:Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino-gun]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]) – note that this image is miscaptioned as “stone pagoda.”]] * [[:en:Ikegami Honmon-ji|Ikegami Honmon-ji]] (located in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], designated an Important Cultural Property) – built in 1828 (Bunsei 11).
=== Indoor Mini-Pagodas === * {{ill|Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|ja|慈光寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|1=Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11494981}} (Saitama Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in 1556 (Tenmon 25). * {{ill|Seikai-ji (Ina City)|ja|性海寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seikai-ji (Ina City)|1=Seikai-ji (Ina City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11491604}} (Aichi Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) * {{ill|Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|ja|長蔵寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|1=Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q113498452}} (Gifu Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) * Honmon-ji (in Tokyo, Important Cultural Property) – built in 1830 (Bunsei 13); this pagoda is contained within the Honmon-ji complex.
=== Stone Pagodas === Stone pagodas occupy a major section of the field of [[Stone Art]]. Some stone pagodas feature carved door-like panels or images of two figures (the Manyo Rulai and Shakyamuni Tathāgata) on the cylindrical body. Regional examples include the {{ill|Kunō-tō|ja|国東塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunō-tō|1=Kunō-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420973}} in {{ill|Kunisaki, Ōita|ja|国東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisaki, Ōita|1=Kunisaki, Ōita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420970}}, Ōita Prefecture, and the {{ill|Akazaki-tō|ja|赤碕塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazaki-tō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]]. * [[:en:Kurama-dera|Kurama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Kyoto, National Treasure) – an inscribed Buddhist scripture tube bearing the inscription of the Hōan era (【1120】) was discovered buried in the soil beneath the tower. It is considered the oldest extant example of a stone pagoda. * Ruins of {{ill|Kande-ji|ja|関寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kande-ji|1=Seki-dera Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655708}}: “Ushi-tō” (Stone Pagoda), (in Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – from the late Heian period; 3.3 m tall with an octagonal roof; the ornamental ring atop the roof was added later. It, together with the Kurama-dera pagoda, Ranks among the oldest. * The ruins of the pagoda at {{ill|Mangan-ji|ja|満願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mangan-ji|1=Mangan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563954}} (in Takashima City, Shiga Prefecture) – from the early Kamakura period; 4 m tall; the ornamental ring was added later. * [[:en:Jōdo-ji|Jōdo-ji (Onomichi)]] Pagoda (in Onomichi, Hiroshima Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the date of the first year of the Kōan era (【1278】); 2.8 m tall; built using rammed earth on a dais. * {{ill|Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|ja|西明寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|1=Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628430}} Pagoda (in Kōra-cho, Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the 2nd year of the Jiagen era (【1304】). * [[:en:Ishiyama-dera|Ishiyama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture) – from the mid-Kamakura period; currently about 2 m tall, though originally estimated at about 3 m. * Ishiyama-dera “Mekakushi-iwa” (in Shiga Prefecture) – from the Heian period. * {{ill|Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|ja|安養寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|1=Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451679}} (with a pagoda attributed to [[:en:Jien|Jien]]) (in Kyoto, Important Cultural Property) – from the mid-Kamakura period; approximately 3 m tall with carvings of the Manyo and Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddhas on the tower body. * [[:en:Rokuharamitsu-ji|Roppōmitsu-ji]], Agoya-tsuka (in Kyoto Prefecture) – from the Kamakura period. * {{ill|Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|ja|長楽寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|1=Chōraku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653105}} Pagoda (in Ota City, Gunma Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in the 2nd year of Kenchi (【1276】).
== References == * Ishida Shigesaku, “Pagoda – Tower and Tower-Ba (Stūba)”, Japanese Art 77, 1972, Shibundo. * Nakani Tō, “Overview of Japanese Pagodas”, 1970, Bunka-dō. * Kawakatsu Masatarō, “Introduction to Stone Art”, 1967, Shakai Shisōsha.

{{Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}} {{nihongo|'''Treasure Pagoda'''|宝塔|hōtō}} has the following two meanings: * It is one architectural style of Buddhist pagodas, referring to a single-storied tower with a cylindrical body. (This article explains this meaning.) * It is also an honorific term for Buddhist pagodas in general. ---- == Form and Significance of the Pagoda == A ''pagoda'' (宝塔, hōtō) is one architectural style of Buddhist pagodas. Although the term “pagoda” is sometimes used honorifically to refer to pagodas in general regardless of form or style, in the terminology of Japanese architectural history it refers specifically to a single-storied pagoda having a cylindrical core (tower body) topped with a flat, square roof<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本石造美術辞典|last=川勝|first=政太郎|date=1978|publisher=東京堂|page=347}}; {{Cite book|title=古建築の基礎知識|last=前久夫|date=1986|publisher=光村推古書院|page=61}}</ref>. The upper portion of the cylinder is rounded off in a style known as “kame-bara” (turtle-belly). A row of ornamental rings (相輪) similar to those on multi-storied pagodas is placed on top of the roof. Pagodas may be constructed of wood, metal, or stone, although extant examples of wooden pagodas are extremely rare. In Japan, the oldest surviving example of a wooden pagoda is a bronze pagoda from the Ikegami Honmon-ji complex (built around the first year of the Hōan era, late Heian period)<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本石造美術辞典|last=川勝|first=政太郎|date=1978|publisher=東京堂|page=347}}</ref>. Note that a two-storied structure in which a flat, circular upper story is mounted on top of an initial flat, square story (known as a “Tahōtō”) is distinguished from the pagoda described here; this distinction is merely conventional.
== Examples of Pagodas == === Wooden Pagodas === [[File:鳳閣寺廟塔02.JPG|thumb|Sample image: a stone pagoda (at {{ill|Hōkaku-ji|ja|鳳閣寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkaku-ji|1=Hōkaku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11675305}} in [[:en:Kurotaki, Nara|Kurotaki Village]], [[:en:Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino-gun]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]) – note that this image is miscaptioned as “stone pagoda.”]] * [[:en:Ikegami Honmon-ji|Ikegami Honmon-ji]] (located in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], designated an Important Cultural Property) – built in 1828 (Bunsei 11).
=== Indoor Mini-Pagodas === * {{ill|Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|ja|慈光寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|1=Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11494981}} (Saitama Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in 1556 (Tenmon 25). * {{ill|Seikai-ji (Ina City)|ja|性海寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seikai-ji (Ina City)|1=Seikai-ji (Ina City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11491604}} (Aichi Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) * {{ill|Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|ja|長蔵寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|1=Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q113498452}} (Gifu Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) * Honmon-ji (in Tokyo, Important Cultural Property) – built in 1830 (Bunsei 13); this pagoda is contained within the Honmon-ji complex.
=== Stone Pagodas === Stone pagodas occupy a major section of the field of [[Stone Art]]. Some stone pagodas feature carved door-like panels or images of two figures (the Manyo Rulai and Shakyamuni Tathāgata) on the cylindrical body. Regional examples include the {{ill|Kunō-tō|ja|国東塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunō-tō|1=Kunō-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420973}} in {{ill|Kunisaki, Ōita|ja|国東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisaki, Ōita|1=Kunisaki, Ōita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420970}}, Ōita Prefecture, and the {{ill|Akazaki-tō|ja|赤碕塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazaki-tō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]]. * [[:en:Kurama-dera|Kurama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Kyoto, National Treasure) – an inscribed Buddhist scripture tube bearing the inscription of the Hōan era (【1120】) was discovered buried in the soil beneath the tower. It is considered the oldest extant example of a stone pagoda. * Ruins of {{ill|Kande-ji|ja|関寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kande-ji|1=Seki-dera Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655708}}: “Ushi-tō” (Stone Pagoda), (in Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – from the late Heian period; 3.3 m tall with an octagonal roof; the ornamental ring atop the roof was added later. It, together with the Kurama-dera pagoda, Ranks among the oldest. * The ruins of the pagoda at {{ill|Mangan-ji|ja|満願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mangan-ji|1=Mangan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563954}} (in Takashima City, Shiga Prefecture) – from the early Kamakura period; 4 m tall; the ornamental ring was added later. * [[:en:Jōdo-ji|Jōdo-ji (Onomichi)]] Pagoda (in Onomichi, Hiroshima Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the date of the first year of the Kōan era (【1278】); 2.8 m tall; built using rammed earth on a dais. * {{ill|Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|ja|西明寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|1=Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628430}} Pagoda (in Kōra-cho, Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the 2nd year of the Jiagen era (【1304】). * [[:en:Ishiyama-dera|Ishiyama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture) – from the mid-Kamakura period; currently about 2 m tall, though originally estimated at about 3 m. * Ishiyama-dera “Mekakushi-iwa” (in Shiga Prefecture) – from the Heian period. * {{ill|Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|ja|安養寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|1=Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451679}} (with a pagoda attributed to [[:en:Jien|Jien]]) (in Kyoto, Important Cultural Property) – from the mid-Kamakura period; approximately 3 m tall with carvings of the Manyo and Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddhas on the tower body. * [[:en:Rokuharamitsu-ji|Roppōmitsu-ji]], Agoya-tsuka (in Kyoto Prefecture) – from the Kamakura period. * {{ill|Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|ja|長楽寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|1=Chōraku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653105}} Pagoda (in Ota City, Gunma Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in the 2nd year of Kenchi (【1276】).
== References == * Ishida Shigesaku, “Pagoda – Tower and Tower-Ba (Stūba)”, Japanese Art 77, 1972, Shibundo. * Nakani Tō, “Overview of Japanese Pagodas”, 1970, Bunka-dō. * Kawakatsu Masatarō, “Introduction to Stone Art”, 1967, Shakai Shisōsha.

It, together with the Kurama-dera pagoda, Ranks among the oldest. * The ruins of the pagoda at {{ill|Mangan-ji|ja|満願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mangan-ji|1=Mangan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563954}} (in Takashima City, Shiga Prefecture) – from the early Kamakura period; 4 m tall; the ornamental ring was added later. * [[:en:Jōdo-ji|Jōdo-ji (Onomichi)]] Pagoda (in Onomichi, Hiroshima Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the date of the first year of the Kōan era (【1278】); 2.8 m tall; built using rammed earth on a dais. * {{ill|Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|ja|西明寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|1=Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628430}} Pagoda (in Kōra-cho, Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the 2nd year of the Jiagen era (【1304】). * [[:en:Ishiyama-dera|Ishiyama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture) – from the mid-Kamakura period; currently about 2 m tall, though originally estimated at about 3 m. * Ishiyama-dera “Mekakushi-iwa” (in Shiga Prefecture) – from the Heian period. * {{ill|Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|ja|安養寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|1=Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451679}} (with a pagoda attributed to [[:en:Jien|Jien]]) (in Kyoto, Important Cultural Property) – from the mid-Kamakura period; approximately 3 m tall with carvings of the Manyo and Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddhas on the tower body. * [[:en:Rokuharamitsu-ji|Roppōmitsu-ji]], Agoya-tsuka (in Kyoto Prefecture) – from the Kamakura period. * {{ill|Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|ja|長楽寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|1=Chōraku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653105}} Pagoda (in Ota City, Gunma Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in the 2nd year of Kenchi (【1276】).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Hōtō]][[ja:宝塔]][[nl:Hōtō]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11452357]] Wikidata name: hōtō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I think Stupa might fit better than Pagoda due to being one level tall but not sure[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:39, 6 April 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}}
{{Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}} {{nihongo|'''Treasure Pagoda'''|宝塔|hōtō}} has the following two meanings: * It is one architectural style of Buddhist pagodas, referring to a single-storied tower with a cylindrical body. (This article explains this meaning.) * It is also an honorific term for Buddhist pagodas in general. ---- == Form and Significance of the Pagoda == A ''pagoda'' (宝塔, hōtō) is one architectural style of Buddhist pagodas. Although the term “pagoda” is sometimes used honorifically to refer to pagodas in general regardless of form or style, in the terminology of Japanese architectural history it refers specifically to a single-storied pagoda having a cylindrical core (tower body) topped with a flat, square roof<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本石造美術辞典|last=川勝|first=政太郎|date=1978|publisher=東京堂|page=347}}; {{Cite book|title=古建築の基礎知識|last=前久夫|date=1986|publisher=光村推古書院|page=61}}</ref>. The upper portion of the cylinder is rounded off in a style known as “kame-bara” (turtle-belly). A row of ornamental rings (相輪) similar to those on multi-storied pagodas is placed on top of the roof. Pagodas may be constructed of wood, metal, or stone, although extant examples of wooden pagodas are extremely rare. In Japan, the oldest surviving example of a wooden pagoda is a bronze pagoda from the Ikegami Honmon-ji complex (built around the first year of the Hōan era, late Heian period)<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本石造美術辞典|last=川勝|first=政太郎|date=1978|publisher=東京堂|page=347}}</ref>. Note that a two-storied structure in which a flat, circular upper story is mounted on top of an initial flat, square story (known as a “Tahōtō”) is distinguished from the pagoda described here; this distinction is merely conventional.
== Examples of Pagodas == === Wooden Pagodas === [[File:鳳閣寺廟塔02.JPG|thumb|Sample image: a stone pagoda (at {{ill|Hōkaku-ji|ja|鳳閣寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkaku-ji|1=Hōkaku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11675305}} in [[:en:Kurotaki, Nara|Kurotaki Village]], [[:en:Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino-gun]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]) – note that this image is miscaptioned as “stone pagoda.”]] * [[:en:Ikegami Honmon-ji|Ikegami Honmon-ji]] (located in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], designated an Important Cultural Property) – built in 1828 (Bunsei 11).
=== Indoor Mini-Pagodas === * {{ill|Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|ja|慈光寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|1=Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11494981}} (Saitama Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in 1556 (Tenmon 25). * {{ill|Seikai-ji (Ina City)|ja|性海寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seikai-ji (Ina City)|1=Seikai-ji (Ina City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11491604}} (Aichi Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) * {{ill|Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|ja|長蔵寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|1=Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q113498452}} (Gifu Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) * Honmon-ji (in Tokyo, Important Cultural Property) – built in 1830 (Bunsei 13); this pagoda is contained within the Honmon-ji complex.
=== Stone Pagodas === Stone pagodas occupy a major section of the field of [[Stone Art]]. Some stone pagodas feature carved door-like panels or images of two figures (the Manyo Rulai and Shakyamuni Tathāgata) on the cylindrical body. Regional examples include the {{ill|Kunō-tō|ja|国東塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunō-tō|1=Kunō-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420973}} in {{ill|Kunisaki, Ōita|ja|国東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisaki, Ōita|1=Kunisaki, Ōita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420970}}, Ōita Prefecture, and the {{ill|Akazaki-tō|ja|赤碕塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazaki-tō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]]. * [[:en:Kurama-dera|Kurama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Kyoto, National Treasure) – an inscribed Buddhist scripture tube bearing the inscription of the Hōan era (【1120】) was discovered buried in the soil beneath the tower. It is considered the oldest extant example of a stone pagoda. * Ruins of {{ill|Kande-ji|ja|関寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kande-ji|1=Seki-dera Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655708}}: “Ushi-tō” (Stone Pagoda), (in Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – from the late Heian period; 3.3 m tall with an octagonal roof; the ornamental ring atop the roof was added later. It, together with the Kurama-dera pagoda, Ranks among the oldest. * The ruins of the pagoda at {{ill|Mangan-ji|ja|満願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mangan-ji|1=Mangan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563954}} (in Takashima City, Shiga Prefecture) – from the early Kamakura period; 4 m tall; the ornamental ring was added later. * [[:en:Jōdo-ji|Jōdo-ji (Onomichi)]] Pagoda (in Onomichi, Hiroshima Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the date of the first year of the Kōan era (【1278】); 2.8 m tall; built using rammed earth on a dais. * {{ill|Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|ja|西明寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|1=Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628430}} Pagoda (in Kōra-cho, Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the 2nd year of the Jiagen era (【1304】). * [[:en:Ishiyama-dera|Ishiyama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture) – from the mid-Kamakura period; currently about 2 m tall, though originally estimated at about 3 m. * Ishiyama-dera “Mekakushi-iwa” (in Shiga Prefecture) – from the Heian period. * {{ill|Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|ja|安養寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|1=Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451679}} (with a pagoda attributed to [[:en:Jien|Jien]]) (in Kyoto, Important Cultural Property) – from the mid-Kamakura period; approximately 3 m tall with carvings of the Manyo and Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddhas on the tower body. * [[:en:Rokuharamitsu-ji|Roppōmitsu-ji]], Agoya-tsuka (in Kyoto Prefecture) – from the Kamakura period. * {{ill|Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|ja|長楽寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|1=Chōraku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653105}} Pagoda (in Ota City, Gunma Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in the 2nd year of Kenchi (【1276】).
== References == * Ishida Shigesaku, “Pagoda – Tower and Tower-Ba (Stūba)”, Japanese Art 77, 1972, Shibundo. * Nakani Tō, “Overview of Japanese Pagodas”, 1970, Bunka-dō. * Kawakatsu Masatarō, “Introduction to Stone Art”, 1967, Shakai Shisōsha.

{{Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}} {{nihongo|'''Treasure Pagoda'''|宝塔|hōtō}} has the following two meanings: * It is one architectural style of Buddhist pagodas, referring to a single-storied tower with a cylindrical body. (This article explains this meaning.) * It is also an honorific term for Buddhist pagodas in general. ---- == Form and Significance of the Pagoda == A ''pagoda'' (宝塔, hōtō) is one architectural style of Buddhist pagodas. Although the term “pagoda” is sometimes used honorifically to refer to pagodas in general regardless of form or style, in the terminology of Japanese architectural history it refers specifically to a single-storied pagoda having a cylindrical core (tower body) topped with a flat, square roof<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本石造美術辞典|last=川勝|first=政太郎|date=1978|publisher=東京堂|page=347}}; {{Cite book|title=古建築の基礎知識|last=前久夫|date=1986|publisher=光村推古書院|page=61}}</ref>. The upper portion of the cylinder is rounded off in a style known as “kame-bara” (turtle-belly). A row of ornamental rings (相輪) similar to those on multi-storied pagodas is placed on top of the roof. Pagodas may be constructed of wood, metal, or stone, although extant examples of wooden pagodas are extremely rare. In Japan, the oldest surviving example of a wooden pagoda is a bronze pagoda from the Ikegami Honmon-ji complex (built around the first year of the Hōan era, late Heian period)<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本石造美術辞典|last=川勝|first=政太郎|date=1978|publisher=東京堂|page=347}}</ref>. Note that a two-storied structure in which a flat, circular upper story is mounted on top of an initial flat, square story (known as a “Tahōtō”) is distinguished from the pagoda described here; this distinction is merely conventional.
== Examples of Pagodas == === Wooden Pagodas === [[File:鳳閣寺廟塔02.JPG|thumb|Sample image: a stone pagoda (at {{ill|Hōkaku-ji|ja|鳳閣寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkaku-ji|1=Hōkaku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11675305}} in [[:en:Kurotaki, Nara|Kurotaki Village]], [[:en:Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino-gun]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]) – note that this image is miscaptioned as “stone pagoda.”]] * [[:en:Ikegami Honmon-ji|Ikegami Honmon-ji]] (located in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], designated an Important Cultural Property) – built in 1828 (Bunsei 11).
=== Indoor Mini-Pagodas === * {{ill|Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|ja|慈光寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|1=Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11494981}} (Saitama Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in 1556 (Tenmon 25). * {{ill|Seikai-ji (Ina City)|ja|性海寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seikai-ji (Ina City)|1=Seikai-ji (Ina City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11491604}} (Aichi Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) * {{ill|Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|ja|長蔵寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|1=Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q113498452}} (Gifu Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) * Honmon-ji (in Tokyo, Important Cultural Property) – built in 1830 (Bunsei 13); this pagoda is contained within the Honmon-ji complex.
=== Stone Pagodas === Stone pagodas occupy a major section of the field of [[Stone Art]]. Some stone pagodas feature carved door-like panels or images of two figures (the Manyo Rulai and Shakyamuni Tathāgata) on the cylindrical body. Regional examples include the {{ill|Kunō-tō|ja|国東塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunō-tō|1=Kunō-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420973}} in {{ill|Kunisaki, Ōita|ja|国東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisaki, Ōita|1=Kunisaki, Ōita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420970}}, Ōita Prefecture, and the {{ill|Akazaki-tō|ja|赤碕塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazaki-tō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]]. * [[:en:Kurama-dera|Kurama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Kyoto, National Treasure) – an inscribed Buddhist scripture tube bearing the inscription of the Hōan era (【1120】) was discovered buried in the soil beneath the tower. It is considered the oldest extant example of a stone pagoda. * Ruins of {{ill|Kande-ji|ja|関寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kande-ji|1=Seki-dera Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655708}}: “Ushi-tō” (Stone Pagoda), (in Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – from the late Heian period; 3.3 m tall with an octagonal roof; the ornamental ring atop the roof was added later. It, together with the Kurama-dera pagoda, Ranks among the oldest. * The ruins of the pagoda at {{ill|Mangan-ji|ja|満願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mangan-ji|1=Mangan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563954}} (in Takashima City, Shiga Prefecture) – from the early Kamakura period; 4 m tall; the ornamental ring was added later. * [[:en:Jōdo-ji|Jōdo-ji (Onomichi)]] Pagoda (in Onomichi, Hiroshima Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the date of the first year of the Kōan era (【1278】); 2.8 m tall; built using rammed earth on a dais. * {{ill|Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|ja|西明寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|1=Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628430}} Pagoda (in Kōra-cho, Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the 2nd year of the Jiagen era (【1304】). * [[:en:Ishiyama-dera|Ishiyama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture) – from the mid-Kamakura period; currently about 2 m tall, though originally estimated at about 3 m. * Ishiyama-dera “Mekakushi-iwa” (in Shiga Prefecture) – from the Heian period. * {{ill|Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|ja|安養寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|1=Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451679}} (with a pagoda attributed to [[:en:Jien|Jien]]) (in Kyoto, Important Cultural Property) – from the mid-Kamakura period; approximately 3 m tall with carvings of the Manyo and Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddhas on the tower body. * [[:en:Rokuharamitsu-ji|Roppōmitsu-ji]], Agoya-tsuka (in Kyoto Prefecture) – from the Kamakura period. * {{ill|Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|ja|長楽寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|1=Chōraku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653105}} Pagoda (in Ota City, Gunma Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in the 2nd year of Kenchi (【1276】).
== References == * Ishida Shigesaku, “Pagoda – Tower and Tower-Ba (Stūba)”, Japanese Art 77, 1972, Shibundo. * Nakani Tō, “Overview of Japanese Pagodas”, 1970, Bunka-dō. * Kawakatsu Masatarō, “Introduction to Stone Art”, 1967, Shakai Shisōsha.
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Prabhutaratna|Manyo Rulai]] * [[:en:Cloisonné|Seven Treasures]] * {{ill|Kunō-tō|ja|国東塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunō-tō|1=Kunō-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420973}} * {{ill|Akagi-tō|ja|赤城塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagi-tō|qq=}} * [[:en:Tahōtō|Tahōtō]]

It, together with the Kurama-dera pagoda, Ranks among the oldest. * The ruins of the pagoda at {{ill|Mangan-ji|ja|満願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mangan-ji|1=Mangan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563954}} (in Takashima City, Shiga Prefecture) – from the early Kamakura period; 4 m tall; the ornamental ring was added later. * [[:en:Jōdo-ji|Jōdo-ji (Onomichi)]] Pagoda (in Onomichi, Hiroshima Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the date of the first year of the Kōan era (【1278】); 2.8 m tall; built using rammed earth on a dais. * {{ill|Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|ja|西明寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|1=Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628430}} Pagoda (in Kōra-cho, Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the 2nd year of the Jiagen era (【1304】). * [[:en:Ishiyama-dera|Ishiyama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture) – from the mid-Kamakura period; currently about 2 m tall, though originally estimated at about 3 m. * Ishiyama-dera “Mekakushi-iwa” (in Shiga Prefecture) – from the Heian period. * {{ill|Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|ja|安養寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|1=Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451679}} (with a pagoda attributed to [[:en:Jien|Jien]]) (in Kyoto, Important Cultural Property) – from the mid-Kamakura period; approximately 3 m tall with carvings of the Manyo and Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddhas on the tower body. * [[:en:Rokuharamitsu-ji|Roppōmitsu-ji]], Agoya-tsuka (in Kyoto Prefecture) – from the Kamakura period. * {{ill|Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|ja|長楽寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|1=Chōraku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653105}} Pagoda (in Ota City, Gunma Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in the 2nd year of Kenchi (【1276】).
== References == * Ishida Shigesaku, “Pagoda – Tower and Tower-Ba (Stūba)”, Japanese Art 77, 1972, Shibundo. * Nakani Tō, “Overview of Japanese Pagodas”, 1970, Bunka-dō. * Kawakatsu Masatarō, “Introduction to Stone Art”, 1967, Shakai Shisōsha.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Hōtō]][[ja:宝塔]][[nl:Hōtō]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Hōtō]][[ja:宝塔]][[nl:Hōtō]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11452357]] Wikidata name: hōtō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:03, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I think Stupa might fit better than Pagoda due to being one level tall but not sure[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 23:39, 6 April 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Referencesの明記|date=2017th year 8月28日 (月) 07:45 (UTC)}}
{{Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}} {{nihongo|'''Treasure Pagoda'''|宝塔|hōtō}} has the following two meanings: * It is one architectural style of Buddhist pagodas, referring to a single-storied tower with a cylindrical body. (This article explains this meaning.) * It is also an honorific term for Buddhist pagodas in general. ---- == Form and Significance of the Pagoda == A ''pagoda'' (宝塔, hōtō) is one architectural style of Buddhist pagodas. Although the term “pagoda” is sometimes used honorifically to refer to pagodas in general regardless of form or style, in the terminology of Japanese architectural history it refers specifically to a single-storied pagoda having a cylindrical core (tower body) topped with a flat, square roof<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本石造美術辞典|last=川勝|first=政太郎|date=1978|publisher=東京堂|page=347}}; {{Cite book|title=古建築の基礎知識|last=前久夫|date=1986|publisher=光村推古書院|page=61}}</ref>. The upper portion of the cylinder is rounded off in a style known as “kame-bara” (turtle-belly). A row of ornamental rings (相輪) similar to those on multi-storied pagodas is placed on top of the roof. Pagodas may be constructed of wood, metal, or stone, although extant examples of wooden pagodas are extremely rare. In Japan, the oldest surviving example of a wooden pagoda is a bronze pagoda from the Ikegami Honmon-ji complex (built around the first year of the Hōan era, late Heian period)<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本石造美術辞典|last=川勝|first=政太郎|date=1978|publisher=東京堂|page=347}}</ref>. Note that a two-storied structure in which a flat, circular upper story is mounted on top of an initial flat, square story (known as a “Tahōtō”) is distinguished from the pagoda described here; this distinction is merely conventional.
== Examples of Pagodas == === Wooden Pagodas === [[File:鳳閣寺廟塔02.JPG|thumb|Sample image: a stone pagoda (at {{ill|Hōkaku-ji|ja|鳳閣寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkaku-ji|1=Hōkaku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11675305}} in [[:en:Kurotaki, Nara|Kurotaki Village]], [[:en:Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino-gun]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]) – note that this image is miscaptioned as “stone pagoda.”]] * [[:en:Ikegami Honmon-ji|Ikegami Honmon-ji]] (located in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], designated an Important Cultural Property) – built in 1828 (Bunsei 11).
=== Indoor Mini-Pagodas === * {{ill|Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|ja|慈光寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|1=Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11494981}} (Saitama Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in 1556 (Tenmon 25). * {{ill|Seikai-ji (Ina City)|ja|性海寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seikai-ji (Ina City)|1=Seikai-ji (Ina City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11491604}} (Aichi Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) * {{ill|Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|ja|長蔵寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|1=Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q113498452}} (Gifu Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) * Honmon-ji (in Tokyo, Important Cultural Property) – built in 1830 (Bunsei 13); this pagoda is contained within the Honmon-ji complex.
=== Stone Pagodas === Stone pagodas occupy a major section of the field of [[Stone Art]]. Some stone pagodas feature carved door-like panels or images of two figures (the Manyo Rulai and Shakyamuni Tathāgata) on the cylindrical body. Regional examples include the {{ill|Kunō-tō|ja|国東塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunō-tō|1=Kunō-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420973}} in {{ill|Kunisaki, Ōita|ja|国東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisaki, Ōita|1=Kunisaki, Ōita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420970}}, Ōita Prefecture, and the {{ill|Akazaki-tō|ja|赤碕塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazaki-tō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]]. * [[:en:Kurama-dera|Kurama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Kyoto, National Treasure) – an inscribed Buddhist scripture tube bearing the inscription of the Hōan era (【1120】) was discovered buried in the soil beneath the tower. It is considered the oldest extant example of a stone pagoda. * Ruins of {{ill|Kande-ji|ja|関寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kande-ji|1=Seki-dera Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655708}}: “Ushi-tō” (Stone Pagoda), (in Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – from the late Heian period; 3.3 m tall with an octagonal roof; the ornamental ring atop the roof was added later. It, together with the Kurama-dera pagoda, Ranks among the oldest. * The ruins of the pagoda at {{ill|Mangan-ji|ja|満願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mangan-ji|1=Mangan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563954}} (in Takashima City, Shiga Prefecture) – from the early Kamakura period; 4 m tall; the ornamental ring was added later. * [[:en:Jōdo-ji|Jōdo-ji (Onomichi)]] Pagoda (in Onomichi, Hiroshima Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the date of the first year of the Kōan era (【1278】); 2.8 m tall; built using rammed earth on a dais. * {{ill|Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|ja|西明寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|1=Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628430}} Pagoda (in Kōra-cho, Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the 2nd year of the Jiagen era (【1304】). * [[:en:Ishiyama-dera|Ishiyama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture) – from the mid-Kamakura period; currently about 2 m tall, though originally estimated at about 3 m. * Ishiyama-dera “Mekakushi-iwa” (in Shiga Prefecture) – from the Heian period. * {{ill|Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|ja|安養寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|1=Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451679}} (with a pagoda attributed to [[:en:Jien|Jien]]) (in Kyoto, Important Cultural Property) – from the mid-Kamakura period; approximately 3 m tall with carvings of the Manyo and Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddhas on the tower body. * [[:en:Rokuharamitsu-ji|Roppōmitsu-ji]], Agoya-tsuka (in Kyoto Prefecture) – from the Kamakura period. * {{ill|Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|ja|長楽寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|1=Chōraku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653105}} Pagoda (in Ota City, Gunma Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in the 2nd year of Kenchi (【1276】).
== References == * Ishida Shigesaku, “Pagoda – Tower and Tower-Ba (Stūba)”, Japanese Art 77, 1972, Shibundo. * Nakani Tō, “Overview of Japanese Pagodas”, 1970, Bunka-dō. * Kawakatsu Masatarō, “Introduction to Stone Art”, 1967, Shakai Shisōsha.

{{Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}} {{nihongo|'''Treasure Pagoda'''|宝塔|hōtō}} has the following two meanings: * It is one architectural style of Buddhist pagodas, referring to a single-storied tower with a cylindrical body. (This article explains this meaning.) * It is also an honorific term for Buddhist pagodas in general. ---- == Form and Significance of the Pagoda == A ''pagoda'' (宝塔, hōtō) is one architectural style of Buddhist pagodas. Although the term “pagoda” is sometimes used honorifically to refer to pagodas in general regardless of form or style, in the terminology of Japanese architectural history it refers specifically to a single-storied pagoda having a cylindrical core (tower body) topped with a flat, square roof<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本石造美術辞典|last=川勝|first=政太郎|date=1978|publisher=東京堂|page=347}}; {{Cite book|title=古建築の基礎知識|last=前久夫|date=1986|publisher=光村推古書院|page=61}}</ref>. The upper portion of the cylinder is rounded off in a style known as “kame-bara” (turtle-belly). A row of ornamental rings (相輪) similar to those on multi-storied pagodas is placed on top of the roof. Pagodas may be constructed of wood, metal, or stone, although extant examples of wooden pagodas are extremely rare. In Japan, the oldest surviving example of a wooden pagoda is a bronze pagoda from the Ikegami Honmon-ji complex (built around the first year of the Hōan era, late Heian period)<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本石造美術辞典|last=川勝|first=政太郎|date=1978|publisher=東京堂|page=347}}</ref>. Note that a two-storied structure in which a flat, circular upper story is mounted on top of an initial flat, square story (known as a “Tahōtō”) is distinguished from the pagoda described here; this distinction is merely conventional.
== Examples of Pagodas == === Wooden Pagodas === [[File:鳳閣寺廟塔02.JPG|thumb|Sample image: a stone pagoda (at {{ill|Hōkaku-ji|ja|鳳閣寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkaku-ji|1=Hōkaku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11675305}} in [[:en:Kurotaki, Nara|Kurotaki Village]], [[:en:Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino-gun]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]) – note that this image is miscaptioned as “stone pagoda.”]] * [[:en:Ikegami Honmon-ji|Ikegami Honmon-ji]] (located in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], designated an Important Cultural Property) – built in 1828 (Bunsei 11).
=== Indoor Mini-Pagodas === * {{ill|Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|ja|慈光寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|1=Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11494981}} (Saitama Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in 1556 (Tenmon 25). * {{ill|Seikai-ji (Ina City)|ja|性海寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seikai-ji (Ina City)|1=Seikai-ji (Ina City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11491604}} (Aichi Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) * {{ill|Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|ja|長蔵寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|1=Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q113498452}} (Gifu Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) * Honmon-ji (in Tokyo, Important Cultural Property) – built in 1830 (Bunsei 13); this pagoda is contained within the Honmon-ji complex.
=== Stone Pagodas === Stone pagodas occupy a major section of the field of [[Stone Art]]. Some stone pagodas feature carved door-like panels or images of two figures (the Manyo Rulai and Shakyamuni Tathāgata) on the cylindrical body. Regional examples include the {{ill|Kunō-tō|ja|国東塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunō-tō|1=Kunō-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420973}} in {{ill|Kunisaki, Ōita|ja|国東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisaki, Ōita|1=Kunisaki, Ōita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420970}}, Ōita Prefecture, and the {{ill|Akazaki-tō|ja|赤碕塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazaki-tō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]]. * [[:en:Kurama-dera|Kurama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Kyoto, National Treasure) – an inscribed Buddhist scripture tube bearing the inscription of the Hōan era (【1120】) was discovered buried in the soil beneath the tower. It is considered the oldest extant example of a stone pagoda. * Ruins of {{ill|Kande-ji|ja|関寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kande-ji|1=Seki-dera Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655708}}: “Ushi-tō” (Stone Pagoda), (in Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – from the late Heian period; 3.3 m tall with an octagonal roof; the ornamental ring atop the roof was added later. It, together with the Kurama-dera pagoda, Ranks among the oldest. * The ruins of the pagoda at {{ill|Mangan-ji|ja|満願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mangan-ji|1=Mangan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563954}} (in Takashima City, Shiga Prefecture) – from the early Kamakura period; 4 m tall; the ornamental ring was added later. * [[:en:Jōdo-ji|Jōdo-ji (Onomichi)]] Pagoda (in Onomichi, Hiroshima Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the date of the first year of the Kōan era (【1278】); 2.8 m tall; built using rammed earth on a dais. * {{ill|Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|ja|西明寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|1=Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628430}} Pagoda (in Kōra-cho, Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the 2nd year of the Jiagen era (【1304】). * [[:en:Ishiyama-dera|Ishiyama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture) – from the mid-Kamakura period; currently about 2 m tall, though originally estimated at about 3 m. * Ishiyama-dera “Mekakushi-iwa” (in Shiga Prefecture) – from the Heian period. * {{ill|Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|ja|安養寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|1=Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451679}} (with a pagoda attributed to [[:en:Jien|Jien]]) (in Kyoto, Important Cultural Property) – from the mid-Kamakura period; approximately 3 m tall with carvings of the Manyo and Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddhas on the tower body. * [[:en:Rokuharamitsu-ji|Roppōmitsu-ji]], Agoya-tsuka (in Kyoto Prefecture) – from the Kamakura period. * {{ill|Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|ja|長楽寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|1=Chōraku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653105}} Pagoda (in Ota City, Gunma Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in the 2nd year of Kenchi (【1276】).
== References == * Ishida Shigesaku, “Pagoda – Tower and Tower-Ba (Stūba)”, Japanese Art 77, 1972, Shibundo. * Nakani Tō, “Overview of Japanese Pagodas”, 1970, Bunka-dō. * Kawakatsu Masatarō, “Introduction to Stone Art”, 1967, Shakai Shisōsha.
== Footnotes == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Prabhutaratna|Manyo Rulai]] * [[:en:Cloisonné|Seven Treasures]] * {{ill|Kunō-tō|ja|国東塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunō-tō|1=Kunō-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420973}} * {{ill|Akagi-tō|ja|赤城塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagi-tō|qq=}} * [[:en:Tahōtō|Tahōtō]]

It, together with the Kurama-dera pagoda, Ranks among the oldest. * The ruins of the pagoda at {{ill|Mangan-ji|ja|満願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mangan-ji|1=Mangan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563954}} (in Takashima City, Shiga Prefecture) – from the early Kamakura period; 4 m tall; the ornamental ring was added later. * [[:en:Jōdo-ji|Jōdo-ji (Onomichi)]] Pagoda (in Onomichi, Hiroshima Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the date of the first year of the Kōan era (【1278】); 2.8 m tall; built using rammed earth on a dais. * {{ill|Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|ja|西明寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|1=Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628430}} Pagoda (in Kōra-cho, Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the 2nd year of the Jiagen era (【1304】). * [[:en:Ishiyama-dera|Ishiyama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture) – from the mid-Kamakura period; currently about 2 m tall, though originally estimated at about 3 m. * Ishiyama-dera “Mekakushi-iwa” (in Shiga Prefecture) – from the Heian period. * {{ill|Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|ja|安養寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|1=Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451679}} (with a pagoda attributed to [[:en:Jien|Jien]]) (in Kyoto, Important Cultural Property) – from the mid-Kamakura period; approximately 3 m tall with carvings of the Manyo and Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddhas on the tower body. * [[:en:Rokuharamitsu-ji|Roppōmitsu-ji]], Agoya-tsuka (in Kyoto Prefecture) – from the Kamakura period. * {{ill|Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|ja|長楽寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|1=Chōraku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653105}} Pagoda (in Ota City, Gunma Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in the 2nd year of Kenchi (【1276】).
== References == * Ishida Shigesaku, “Pagoda – Tower and Tower-Ba (Stūba)”, Japanese Art 77, 1972, Shibundo. * Nakani Tō, “Overview of Japanese Pagodas”, 1970, Bunka-dō. * Kawakatsu Masatarō, “Introduction to Stone Art”, 1967, Shakai Shisōsha.

{{Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace}} {{nihongo|'''Treasure Pagoda'''|宝塔|hōtō}} has the following two meanings: * It is one architectural style of Buddhist pagodas, referring to a single-storied tower with a cylindrical body. (This article explains this meaning.) * It is also an honorific term for Buddhist pagodas in general. ---- == Form and Significance of the Pagoda == A ''pagoda'' (宝塔, hōtō) is one architectural style of Buddhist pagodas. Although the term “pagoda” is sometimes used honorifically to refer to pagodas in general regardless of form or style, in the terminology of Japanese architectural history it refers specifically to a single-storied pagoda having a cylindrical core (tower body) topped with a flat, square roof<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本石造美術辞典|last=川勝|first=政太郎|date=1978|publisher=東京堂|page=347}}; {{Cite book|title=古建築の基礎知識|last=前久夫|date=1986|publisher=光村推古書院|page=61}}</ref>. The upper portion of the cylinder is rounded off in a style known as “kame-bara” (turtle-belly). A row of ornamental rings (相輪) similar to those on multi-storied pagodas is placed on top of the roof. Pagodas may be constructed of wood, metal, or stone, although extant examples of wooden pagodas are extremely rare. In Japan, the oldest surviving example of a wooden pagoda is a bronze pagoda from the Ikegami Honmon-ji complex (built around the first year of the Hōan era, late Heian period)<ref>{{Cite book|title=日本石造美術辞典|last=川勝|first=政太郎|date=1978|publisher=東京堂|page=347}}</ref>. Note that a two-storied structure in which a flat, circular upper story is mounted on top of an initial flat, square story (known as a “Tahōtō”) is distinguished from the pagoda described here; this distinction is merely conventional.
== Examples of Pagodas == === Wooden Pagodas === [[File:鳳閣寺廟塔02.JPG|thumb|Sample image: a stone pagoda (at {{ill|Hōkaku-ji|ja|鳳閣寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkaku-ji|1=Hōkaku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11675305}} in [[:en:Kurotaki, Nara|Kurotaki Village]], [[:en:Yoshino District, Nara|Yoshino-gun]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]) – note that this image is miscaptioned as “stone pagoda.”]] * [[:en:Ikegami Honmon-ji|Ikegami Honmon-ji]] (located in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], designated an Important Cultural Property) – built in 1828 (Bunsei 11).
=== Indoor Mini-Pagodas === * {{ill|Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|ja|慈光寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|1=Jikō-ji (Tokigawamachi, Saitama)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11494981}} (Saitama Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in 1556 (Tenmon 25). * {{ill|Seikai-ji (Ina City)|ja|性海寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seikai-ji (Ina City)|1=Seikai-ji (Ina City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11491604}} (Aichi Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) * {{ill|Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|ja|長蔵寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|1=Chōzō-ji (Minō City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q113498452}} (Gifu Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) * Honmon-ji (in Tokyo, Important Cultural Property) – built in 1830 (Bunsei 13); this pagoda is contained within the Honmon-ji complex.
=== Stone Pagodas === Stone pagodas occupy a major section of the field of [[Stone Art]]. Some stone pagodas feature carved door-like panels or images of two figures (the Manyo Rulai and Shakyamuni Tathāgata) on the cylindrical body. Regional examples include the {{ill|Kunō-tō|ja|国東塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunō-tō|1=Kunō-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420973}} in {{ill|Kunisaki, Ōita|ja|国東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisaki, Ōita|1=Kunisaki, Ōita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420970}}, Ōita Prefecture, and the {{ill|Akazaki-tō|ja|赤碕塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akazaki-tō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori Prefecture]]. * [[:en:Kurama-dera|Kurama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Kyoto, National Treasure) – an inscribed Buddhist scripture tube bearing the inscription of the Hōan era (【1120】) was discovered buried in the soil beneath the tower. It is considered the oldest extant example of a stone pagoda. * Ruins of {{ill|Kande-ji|ja|関寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kande-ji|1=Seki-dera Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655708}}: “Ushi-tō” (Stone Pagoda), (in Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – from the late Heian period; 3.3 m tall with an octagonal roof; the ornamental ring atop the roof was added later. It, together with the Kurama-dera pagoda, Ranks among the oldest. * The ruins of the pagoda at {{ill|Mangan-ji|ja|満願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mangan-ji|1=Mangan-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563954}} (in Takashima City, Shiga Prefecture) – from the early Kamakura period; 4 m tall; the ornamental ring was added later. * [[:en:Jōdo-ji|Jōdo-ji (Onomichi)]] Pagoda (in Onomichi, Hiroshima Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the date of the first year of the Kōan era (【1278】); 2.8 m tall; built using rammed earth on a dais. * {{ill|Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|ja|西明寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|1=Saimei-ji (Kōra-cho, Shiga)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11628430}} Pagoda (in Kōra-cho, Shiga Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – inscribed with the 2nd year of the Jiagen era (【1304】). * [[:en:Ishiyama-dera|Ishiyama-dera]] Stone Pagoda (in Ōtsu, Shiga Prefecture) – from the mid-Kamakura period; currently about 2 m tall, though originally estimated at about 3 m. * Ishiyama-dera “Mekakushi-iwa” (in Shiga Prefecture) – from the Heian period. * {{ill|Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|ja|安養寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|1=Anyō-ji (Higashiyama, Kyoto)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451679}} (with a pagoda attributed to [[:en:Jien|Jien]]) (in Kyoto, Important Cultural Property) – from the mid-Kamakura period; approximately 3 m tall with carvings of the Manyo and Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddhas on the tower body. * [[:en:Rokuharamitsu-ji|Roppōmitsu-ji]], Agoya-tsuka (in Kyoto Prefecture) – from the Kamakura period. * {{ill|Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|ja|長楽寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōraku-ji (Ota City)|1=Chōraku-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653105}} Pagoda (in Ota City, Gunma Prefecture, Important Cultural Property) – built in the 2nd year of Kenchi (【1276】).
== References == * Ishida Shigesaku, “Pagoda – Tower and Tower-Ba (Stūba)”, Japanese Art 77, 1972, Shibundo. * Nakani Tō, “Overview of Japanese Pagodas”, 1970, Bunka-dō. * Kawakatsu Masatarō, “Introduction to Stone Art”, 1967, Shakai Shisōsha.
